data.title: Beam Therapeutics: Pivoting From Platform To Execution Stage
data.description:
Beam Therapeutics Inc. (BEAM) is a clinically staged biotechnology company focused on development of precision genetic medicines through a unique base gene editing technology. Recently, Beam shares have surged as progress was reported prior to the company presenting at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference on Tuesday evening (January 13, 2026, at 5:15 p.m. PST).

This has shifted the narrative on Beam from a promising "platform-driven" story to a more defined thesis that will be driven by "regulatory execution." Specifically, the company's two most advanced programs, BEAM-302 and BEAM-101 (now Risto-cel) are progressing rapidly, with the former advancing toward registration-enabling studies and the latter to a U.S. Biologics Licensing Application (BLA) Submission as early as the end of 2026.

Risks remain, despite greater regulatory clarity. In particular, the commercial landscape for Risto-cel in Sickle Cell Disease (SCD) is crowded with CRISPR Therapeutics' (CRSP) Casgevy and bluebird bio's Lyfgenia competing with ex vivo therapies. Beam is prioritizing its "next-generation" therapy as a best-in-class ex vivo therapy, with the goal of winning market share following FDA approval.

BEAM-302 and future programs are pursuing the "holy grail" of in vivo gene editing through a single infusion that does not require a lengthy ex vivo process. Now with the FDA offering accelerated approval on the condition of robust biomarker data in a larger ~50 patient cohort, this paradigm appears closer. Therefore, this development presents a more robust and secure pathway for Beam, supporting a Buy rating.

Beam Therapeutics: BEAM-101 (Risto-Cel) Accelerating Toward a BLA

Risto-cel (formerly known as BEAM-101) is an investigational autologous cell therapy and data supporting its potential "best-in-class" status were presented at the American Society of Hematology (ASH) annual meeting in December.

To summarize the clinical data briefly, Risto-cel demonstrated:

  • Robust increases in protective hemoglobin (HbF), with patients achieving mean HbF levels above 60%.
  • Pathologic hemoglobin S (HbS) was reduced, with patients maintaining HbS levels below 40%.
  • This led to a durable reduction in anemia for up to 20 months.

Unlike CRISPR-Cas9, which causes double-strand DNA breaks during editing, Beam's base editor converts a single letter. This theoretically reduces the risk of unwanted chromosomal translocations.

The commercial future is somewhat clouded by the already crowded landscape in SCD. Casgevy and Lyfgenia are already vying for market share, while Risto-cel is moving toward BLA submission by the end of 2026. This sets Beam up for a potential commercial launch some time in 2027.

Beam Therapeutics: BEAM-302 - The "In Vivo" Game Changer

While SCD provides Beam with its first commercial opportunity, the upside case for the company rests on BEAM-302, an in vivo therapy for the treatment of Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency (AATD). Unlike Risto-cel, which requires a complex transplant procedure, BEAM-302 is delivered via lipid nanoparticle (LNP), offering the 'holy grail' of gene editing in via a single IV infusion.

The path forward for BEAM-302 was significantly derisked this week, with the FDA offering a path forward for accelerated approval. Further, the agency has agreed to evaluate the drug based on biomarker endpoints, specifically the demonstration of successful gene editing. This could accelerate the process significantly, as a much smaller number of patients may achieve this goal, rather than needing more patients over more time to demonstrate clinical benefit.

Severe AATD impacts >100,000 individuals in the U.S., presenting them with limited treatment options. At the root of the disease is a single point mutation. Beam has already demonstrated via biomarkers the clinical benefit of BEAM-302 in a small number of patients. >25 patients have been successfully dosed with >50 planned to meet the required patient threshold.

With the regulatory requirements now clear, Beam is focusing its attention toward execution in 2026. Beam expects to share data from the expanded cohort of patients in AATD in 2026, a critical catalyst for the stock. Risks remain; recently shares of in vivo editing competitor Intellia (NTLA) were hit hard after a patient succumbed to liver injury. Unforeseen risks can surface as the number of patients increases, but Beam has a clear path forward that positions the company for success in 2026.

Conclusion

Beam Therapeutics is undergoing a successful pivot from a platform development story to an execution-driven investment strategy. With a defined regulatory pathway for Risto-cel in late-2026 and a potential accelerated approval pathway for BEAM-302, there are now two credible shots on goal in the coming year, greatly derisking the investment thesis.

Financially speaking, Beam has a long runway with $1.075B in cash, equivalents and short-term investments, with additional liquidity from potential milestones (>$1B). This runway, extending into 2029, provides ample liquidity for the company to pursue its commercialization and clinical development targets.

While the SCD market is crowded, the real value driver is the "holy grail" of in vivo gene editing that BEAM-302 may demonstrate. If successful, this unlocks a host of potential therapies following successful implementation of Beam's editing platform. This setup justifies a Buy rating for investors looking for exposure in the potential high-growth gene-editing space.

data.title: XTN: Avoid This Equal-Weight Transportation ETF In 2026
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Investors should avoid the State Street SPDR S&P Transportation ETF (XTN) in 2026 and instead opt for the cap-weighted version of its Index, represented by the iShares Transportation Average ETF (IYT). As I'll explain, XTN's equal-weighting scheme systematically leads to a lower-quality, higher-risk portfolio of transportation stocks. Furthermore, XTN's earnings growth and valuation statistics are unattractive, leaving investors with few compelling fundamental reasons to own it. As a result, I've assigned XTN a "sell" rating.

XTN Overview

XTN currently has a 0.35% expense ratio and $214M in AUM. The fund launched on January 26, 2011, and seeks to track the S&P Transportation Select Industry Index, which holds stocks of all size segments in equal weight. As of January 10, 2026, XTN's largest sub-industry exposures were in Cargo Ground Transportation (34%), Passenger Airlines (25%), and Air Freight & Logistics (17%). Readers can view the full methodology document here.

XTN's top ten holdings are listed below, which together comprise 28% of the fund. Due to its equal weighting scheme, XTN's weighted average market cap is $24.8B, well below IYT's $82.4B, and according to my ETF Overlap Tool, the two overlap by only 52%. Please note that IYT holds the same 44 stocks as XTN, but it Index weights stocks based on their float-adjusted market caps, subject to certain constraints for those with allocations above 4.5%.

XTN Performance Summary

XTN's historical performance has been characterized by large gains and steep losses. To illustrate, its best one-year total return was 103.42% between April 2020 and March 2021. At the same time, XTN experienced several significant drawdowns, including the 27.14% decline for the period ending April 2025, from which it has yet to recover.

It's true that XTN's annualized returns between February 2011 and December 2025 were superior to IYT's (9.98% vs. 9.56%), but the more meaningful takeaway is that XTN is more sensitive to changes in market conditions. Both are cyclical ETFs, but IYT has realized much less volatility to the point where its risk-adjusted returns, as measured by the Sharpe and Sortino Ratios, were actually stronger than XTN's.

XTN's Structural Weakness: Low Quality

Companies operating in the transportation sector are very capital-intensive, making capital efficiency ratios like EBIT margins, free cash flow margins, and return on total capital crucial indicators of quality. In contrast, measures like EBITDA margins ignore capital intensity. We see this in the table below for XTN's top 15 holdings, which highlights that XTN's weighted average EBIT margins are roughly half of its EBITDA margins (8.81% vs. 15.32%).

While both ETFs exhibit a similar difference between their respective EBITDA and EBIT margins, it's the absolute value of those figures that ultimately drives earnings risk and sensitivity. XTN's 1.60% free cash flow margins highlight how profitability almost evaporates when depreciation, amortization, and capital requirements are considered. These margins leave little room for error and lead to XTN being highly sensitive to even small changes in actual or expected earnings growth. Meanwhile, IYT's 8.05% free cash flow margins suggest it's on much better footing fundamentally.

In the Passenger Airlines sub-industry, where XTN is overweight by 8%, increases in costs that are difficult to predict (e.g., fuel) also increase volatility. Average EBIT and free cash flow margins in this sub-industry are only 4.59% and 0.86%, whereas IYT's cap-weighted approach increases those figures to 6.85% and 2.34%, respectively. It's one example where a cap-weighted approach can contribute to a higher-quality portfolio.

XTN's inferior quality is further supported by its lower 4.93% weighted return on total capital ratio. Importantly, it's not driven by leverage, since both funds have similar long-term-debt-to-total capital ratios (0.42 vs. 0.44). Instead, IYT simply overweights transportation stocks with stronger underlying operations, and this is the reason for its more stable historical return profile.

XTN's Growth And Value Mix Is Also Weak

XTN also has an inferior valuation and growth mix compared to IYT. As shown below, the fund trades with a 22.32x trailing P/E and a 21.39x forward P/E, which are about five and three points higher than IYT. Furthermore, XTN's historical three-year sales growth rate is 3% less, and looking forward to the next twelve months, consensus EPS growth for XTN is just 3.08% vs. 13.91% for IYT. These differences are remarkable and demonstrate the substantial impact a weighting scheme can have on an industry.

Primary Risk To Thesis

The primary risk to my thesis is that I have underestimated the "turnaround story" for some of the transportation industry's worst performers. Although its 3.08% forward EPS growth rate is low compared to IYT, it represents a big improvement from its -9.11% 3Y EPS CAGR, and there's a chance that most of the negative sentiment is already priced in. We are seeing some early signs of this, too, as the table below highlights how XTN's top four holdings are all small/mid-cap stocks, which the fund would overweight relative to IYT.

On a weighted average basis, XTN's components have gained 15.37% over the last six months vs. 11.17% for IYT, confirming that XTN's short-term price momentum is stronger. My thesis would be invalidated if this trend continues, but that's not my expectation. In an efficient market, momentum should be supported by improving fundamentals, and the table above highlights how several of XTN's top performers had low or negative surprises last quarter.

That includes Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX), which missed expectations by 13.15% last quarter despite projecting confidence in a solid fourth quarter. Still, per Seeking Alpha's Earnings Insights into the company's Q3 earnings call, "uncertainty" was a frequently used word, which sums up my opinion about the industry.

Investment Recommendation

I rate XTN a "sell" due to its high sensitivity to earnings changes, weak operational efficiency, and unattractive mix of growth and value. Though its returns distribution profile indicates substantial gains are possible, the risks outweigh the rewards, and interested investors in the transportation industry should opt for the cap-weighted IYT instead.

data.title: PPA: Increased Defense Spending Make This ETF A Buy
data.description:
I have determined that, for the Good of our Country, especially in these very troubled and dangerous times, our Military Budget for the year 2027 should not be $1 Trillion Dollars, but rather $1.5 Trillion Dollars. This will allow us to build the “Dream Military” that we have long been entitled to and, more importantly, that will keep us SAFE and SECURE, regardless of foe. – President Trump.

I rate Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA) as a Buy, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy-and-hold investments. PPA has a long history of outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), holding the best Top 10 Defense & Aerospace companies. Based on future government spending plans, PPA is in an excellent position to outperform, in my view.

This week, President Trump proposed a $1.5 trillion defense budget for 2027, a large increase from the $901 billion in 2026. Over the past few months, the U.S. has intensified its military actions with calls to increase spending on both weapons and space exploration. Specifically, the U.S. has dropped bombs on Iran to destroy their nuclear ambitions, attacked Venezuela capturing the country’s President and wife, and retaliated in Syria for random terrorist acts against U.S. military personnel.

President Trump said we live in “troubled and dangerous times,” making significant threats with intention of using the U.S. military including defense of protestors in Iran, further attacks on Venezuela to defend democracy, and the use of military in the war on drugs in Venezuela and possibly Mexico. President Trump has also called for massive battle ships, “Trump Class” that would bring overwhelming force on the seas. Finally, the President has called for the construction of an Iron Dome, similar to the one used in Israel.

Given his actions within the first year of returning to office, government officials and defense contractors are taking him very seriously, in my opinion.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the ETF are:

  • The U.S. is currently deploying the military, using weapons, and has threatened to escalate in certain regions.
  • The President has called on Congress to increase spending on Defense and Aerospace.
  • ETF has a long history of beating the S&P 500 Index as a benchmark.
  • Strong forward metrics of top 10 holdings
  • The U.S. realizes it's in a race for superior weapons and space ambitions with China and wants to succeed as a matter of national security.
  • U.S. support for space exploration (Artemis Project) is intensifying.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the ETF a Buy 4.45 (as of 1/12/2026), which supports my rating.

PPA Holds the Top Aerospace & Defense Stocks

According to Invesco’s website, the fund is

“based on the SPADE™ Defense Index (Index). The Fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. The Index is designed to identify a group of companies involved in the development, manufacturing, operations and support of US defense, homeland security, and aerospace operations. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.”

The top 10 holdings account for 59.4% of the ETF. A quick glance at the companies shows they are all large, well-established companies that have been in business for a long time. They all have government contracts lasting for years, which can progress over multiple President’s terms.

PPA Outperforms the S&P 500 Index as a Benchmark

An investment of $10,000 in PPA, with dividends reinvested, over the past 10 years would be worth $50,790 today outperforming the S&P 500 Index as represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) with $36,180.

Valuation of Top 10 Holdings

The chart below is in order of the top 10 holdings in the ETF. Notice some extraordinary 5- and 10-year total return growth rates. Within the top 10, almost all except Boeing pay a dividend, which have grown on average 7% per year over the past 5 years. The consecutive years in paying dividends is extraordinary, in my view, showing an average of 25-30 years. Finally, notice the market caps of these enormous companies. Most are about $100 billion.

These major companies have been in the news lately with new orders, contracts, and exciting announcements of upcoming programs supported by the U.S. government. Here are a few of the headlines:

The Boeing Company (BA), after having some operational issues, BA is now a favored stock thanks to its opportunity to sell planes to countries trying to balance their trade deficit. Among the many companies placing orders, Delta Air Lines just ordered 60 Boeing 787 Dreamliners.

General Electric Aerospace (GE) – after splitting into various companies, GE is now focused on engines, winning contracts from such buyers as Saudi Group for the new 787 Dreamliners.

RTX Corporation (RTX) – RTX is the world's largest aerospace and defense company and was recently awarded a $438 million contract by the FAA to support the Radar System Replacement program.

Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) welcomed Secretary of War Pete Hegseth to the Fort Worth, Texas, manufacturing facility on January 12th, the site of F-35 Lightning II aircraft.

Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) – was awarded the U.S. Marine Corps’ Marine Air-Ground Task Force Uncrewed Expeditionary Tactical Aircraft (MUX TACAIR) Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA).

General Dynamics Corporation (GD) was awarded a contract worth $988 million on January 12th to improve systems to modernize the U.S. Navy.

L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX) announced a partnership with the Department of War to ramp up its ability to produce solid rocket motors that fuel missiles for the U.S. and allies.

PPA Outperforms Other Aerospace & Defense ETFs

When comparing PPA to peers, the fund stands out for having the highest price appreciation at 159.1%, greater diversification with 62 holdings vs. an average of 46. Surprisingly, it has the lowest turnover of the four suggesting the portfolio managers have chosen the best securities and are sticking with them long term. Note the ETFs pay very little in the way of dividends, paid on a quarterly basis. Two of the funds including PPA don’t pay one any longer.

iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA) is the largest of the funds as ranked by assets under management (AUM). Achieving the second-highest price performance at 154%, ITA has a strong concentration of 75.3% of assets in the top 10, with only 46 stocks in its portfolio. PPA is about half its size.

State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) pays a small dividend, achieved strong but the lowest 5-year price performance at 146.4%.

State Street SPDR S&P Kensho Final Frontiers ETF (ROKT) is the smallest fund with only $49.6 million in assets, however it has shown dramatic growth in the past as individual stocks chosen correctly can have a larger impact on performance. ROKT also pays a tiny dividend on a quarterly basis.

Over the past 5 years, PPA outperforms peers rising 162.7%, almost tripling.

Given a longer period of 10 years, taking out ROKT, PPA ranks second behind State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF - XAR at 495.2%.

Outlook

President Trump has signaled increased spending for the U.S. Defense and Aerospace industries and expected to follow through on the execution of the plan. In fact, the U.S. military has executed missions dropping bombs on Iran, Venezuela, and Syria, threatening to do more. Therefore, the outlook for the industry has just received a boost of support that is unlikely to slow down, in my view.

Risks To Rating

While President Trump has signaled a passion for boosting the military and supporting the space race, he has demonstrated an ability to flip-flop on his decisions. Earlier in his term, there was a focus on spending cuts which would reduce the budget deficit. After trying to peacefully stop wars, it appears the President has lost his patience, choosing to using force to be more persuasive. The biggest risk I see is that our adversaries retreat and President Trump goes back into cost-cutting mode, reducing the enthusiasm behind defense spending.

Bottom Line

I rate PPA a Buy based on the President’s announcement of increasing defense spending to $1.5 trillion by 2027 and support for the U.S. space ambitions (Project Artemis). The most important factors behind this rating include the fact that the U.S. is currently deploying the military in acts of war, using weapons, and threatening to escalate in certain regions.

Beyond the immediate geopolitical landscape, the ETF has a long history of beating the S&P 500 Index. All the top ten holdings are profitable companies with strong 5- and 10-year total returns, with over 25 years of paying dividends.

The risk to rating is the President changes his mind and backs out from spending. Based on the recent actions, this is unlikely to occur in my view, as foreign countries are ramping up their defenses, including adversary countries such as China, Russia, and Iran.

data.title: GSI Technology: A Speculative Buy Scaling To Profitability
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I’ve held off from covering too many AI stocks because there is so much news coming out that feels almost daily that shifts the market sentiment. It’s a real roller coaster. Today is an exception, though, with GSI Technology (GSIT) which is starting to develop what could be a revolutionary technology that cuts down on energy usage and eliminates the need for GPUs and CPUs in certain environments.

It’s a speculative buy, but I think not having exposure would leave a lot of potential on the table. The balance sheet is healthy, there is a clear path towards profitability through scaling, and if I’m proven wrong in my forecast, there is a lot of further upside. I’m rating the stock a Buy.

Business Model - In The Scaling Stage

In their financial reporting GSIT describes itself as a:

We are a provider of high-performance semiconductor memory solutions for in-place associative computing applications in high growth markets such as artificial intelligence and high-performance computing, including natural language processing and computer vision.

This covers the legacy business of GSIT which consists of SRAM memory chips. Most people know about RAM and even DRAM as well. The main difference here with SRAM is that the memory chip sits inside the networking equipment, meaning that the data processing is done “in-house,” so to speak. It often means higher costs, but the trade off is that it’s far more reliable and has a faster response. This is all because it eliminates the need for constant refreshes that RAM and DRAM require.

GSIT is a fabless company, meaning it only designs the chips and then outsources the work to TSMC (TSM), for example, to produce the chips. Right now the only revenues that GSIT generates are from this legacy business. But 'legacy' doesn't quite do it justice, seeing as the revenues grew by 38% YoY based on the first 6 months of 2025. 41% if based on the Q2 figures.

Industry reports are mixed on what the growth for SRAM will be over the next several years. Some are very bullish and actually mimic the same momentum that GSIT has posted recently.

Over the past 6 years, revenues have fallen sharply for GSIT, nearly 50% from 2019 levels. With this, the operating losses have also widened to $5.3 million during the first 6 months of 2025. But, through this, the gross margins have actually held up very well. This suggests the success of GSIT is entirely tied to its ability to scale its sales pipelines more than anything else. Operating expenses are actually down since 2019, from $16.963 million to $12.547 million (6M basis).

In October of 2025, the company raised $50 million in equity. The annualized operating loss is $10.726 million, which suggests this helped provide a runway of 4.66 more years of operations. Part of the reason why I’m not that concerned with GSIT not yet producing a profit is because its balance sheet, for instance, is incredibly healthy with no traditional debt. On top of this, 21% of the shares are held by insiders, and around 15% by institutions.

Associative Process Unit - APU, The New Growth Opportunity

Having covered the legacy business, we can move on to the very reason GSIT's stock price has risen by over 160% the past 12 months. You see, back in October 2025, a very favorable report came out that analyzed the current GEMINI-1 APU unit that GSIT actually has in production.

The segment aims to solve one of the most important issues that AI companies are facing. Energy costs. When there is a massive amount of data that needs to be moved back and forth between GPUs and CPUs the energy costs are immense. The APU solves this by doing the data processing in-memory or “compute-in-memory” instead. This eliminates the need for separate memories and makes a request that could take minutes to go down to microseconds.

The main target markets that GSIT has are edge AI. This includes drones, military vehicles, or robotics. An APU offers an advantage where it eliminates the need for a GPU or a CPU. Take a drone, for example; not needing these components both brings down the production cost and the performance as well. Military vehicles too. When speed can be the difference between life and death, having the fastest is essential. With President Trump also pushing to increase the US military budget by over 50% to $1.5 trillion, means capital could be flooding into this space.

We shouldn't be confusing GSIT with being a competitor to NVIDIA (NVDA) or Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) in the data center space. It’s too competitive and GSIT would have no chance of disrupting right now no matter how good its technology might be. I say this because there are so many orders already for NVIDIA’s chips, GSIT doesn't have the contracts with TSMC or the capacity to make a meaningful difference here. It makes more sense to focus on edge applications like drones and military vehicles where growing market share is likely easier. As time goes on I can see these APUs being used in data centers, but that’s not what the current investment thesis I have is based on.

with a fully optimized version available in the first half of calendar 2026. This algorithm would be for defense applications such as drones, satellites and other military vehicles.

In the most recent earnings call GSIT outlined the roadmap to production for the APU segment, citing H1 2026 to include the first shipments.

We are embarking on the journey towards a major milestone, the tape-out of Plato chip in early calendar 2027.

The Plato chip is the third-generation APU chip which is aimed specifically at the large-language models. The use case mostly includes embedded AI applications in smartphones or autonomous vehicles. It marks a small shift for GSIT as it broadened its end markets from military and drones to other industries.

There are no mentions of what the revenues will look like here, and management is being vague, I think, because it’s still pre-shipment. But based on the $6.8 billion earlier stated TAM figure in 2025, if GSIT just gets 0.5% of this share, it could translate to $34 million in annual revenues. This would be transformative for the business and justify the jump the share price has done.

The Cornell Study - A Potential Gamechanger

In October of 2025 Cornell University found that the technology that GSIT has in its APU SRAM could be a game changer in the world of AI. Its performance is faster, and its energy usage is far lower. These are the key metrics that customers value. If data centers can push down energy costs, which are estimated to be the largest expense line, with some estimates at 46% of all costs. The second line being speed. As discussed earlier, what differentiates GSIT is that the processing is done in memory, which eliminates the need to move data back and forth. This adds to the energy costs of course but also means AI companies like OpenAI or Grok can train their models both faster and cheaper, potentially being the missing link that investors are looking for as to how these ventures could eventually turn a profit. But think of this as more of a long, long-term opportunity, not a near-term one. NVDA and AMD are too dominant in this space for GSIT to enter and make a difference.

One of the key findings in the Cornell study was the latency breakdown for RAG retrievals. This table shows the time spent searching a massive database to find the return statement. The bottleneck is the calculation distance. It shows that without optimization the time for a 200GB database was 527.9 ms, but with optimization it fell to 74.6 ms, being 7x faster.

This directly proves that the tech GSIT has works and is faster than what is currently readily available on the market right now. Compute heavy searches are now something far more manageable.

The second important figure was the energy usage. For a 200GB database the energy usage was 686.7 joules for a GPU, whilst for a APU it was only 12.6 joules That is only 1.8% of the energy usage of GPUs. It completely revolutionizes the cost profile for data centers if- and it's a big if—GSIT is able to expand into this market in the future.

Pre-Profit, But Getting There Quickly Through Scaling

Going back to the current business of GSIT. It guided for $6 - $6.8 million in revenues for the third quarter as it advanced efforts with GEMINI-II. Annualized, this implies around $25.6 million in net revenues. With a market cap of $276 million, it means GSIT trades at 10.7x sales. I think a 25% top line growth rate is well within reason if GSIT starts shipping out in H1 2026, and more in H2.

Based on this table, I see GSIT reaching $10.4 million in net income by 2030 if it can continue to scale and the APUs are well-received on the market. This values shares at 26.5x 2030 earnings. It’s still a buy for me because of the upside that still might not be baked into the model here. With the way the AI industry has been moving so far there seems to be no lack of capital to throw at new technology. If companies start getting desperate to generate a profit they might turn to GSIT in larger masses. Because of it being fabless it also means this demand can be translated if TSMC has the capacity or opens up more.

There is a clear path towards profitability I think as it’s all tied to increasing the sales pipelines and this is exactly what the APUs will be doing for the company. I’m a buyer of course, but I’m not selling the house and getting more shares here. Having a smaller percentage of exposure seems like the best way forward.

Risks

The main risks I’ve identified with GSIT are clearly outlined in the SEC Filings. GSIT mentions how it has issues raising the average selling price per unit, the unit in this case being the SRAM memory chips of course. Because of this GSIT is inadvertently more impacted by downturns in the semiconductor industry. There is a level of cyclicality which I think is tied to access to cheap capital. Periods where interest rates are lower often seem to coincide with periods of more demand in the industry.

The average selling price of our products has increased or remained unchanged in recent years. However, as is typical in the semiconductor industry, the selling prices of our products has historically declined over the life of the product.

The risk this presents is that GSIT will see far more aggressive swings in net revenues. It can’t pass on some of this by raising prices because then it would most likely lose out on market share instead.

can also be affected by our ability to increase production through the availability of increased wafer fabrication capacity from TSMC, our wafer supplier

The second risk is a consequence of the business model GSIT has. Being a fabless business it means that sales are entirely dependent on the capacity it has with TSMC. The company is under immense pressure to increase capacity as it’s one of just a few factories in the world able to supply. Back in November the company mentioned how even after investments it’s still falling about 3x short of the advanced-node demand, tied to AI demand. I would make the argument that the success of GSIT is in large part tied to the success and access to capacity of TSMC. This means one of the main risks of GSIT is out of its control. To some investors this might be the final straw. Personally, I’m still comfortable buying because of the upside in the developing APU segment.

The Full Picture

The market has clearly taken notice to GSIT with how the stock moved after the Cornell report last October. It’s now up to management to ensure that the shipment for GEMINI-II starts in H1 2026 and is able to scale further. Ironically the APUs might solve a bottleneck with data processing, GSIT itself faces a potential bottleneck with TSMC if capacity isn't available at the required scale. This might be a short-term setback, but not something that puts me off from opening a position in the stock in correlation with this article. I'm rating shares a Buy.
